title: ‘Cocaine Bear’ and the Top Five Cocaine Movies
format: Sean and Amanda sit stone-faced through 'Cocaine Bear,' then each count down five cocaine movies to a shared No. 1. Sean closes with a long interview with 'Turning Red' director Domee Shi.

segments.heading: ‘Cocaine Bear’
segments.note: Elizabeth Banks directs the bear-on-blow movie

films.title: Cocaine Bear
films.note: Loosely drawn from the real 1985 black bear that ate a smuggler's dropped duffel of cocaine in northern Georgia. Elizabeth Banks directs from a Lord and Miller production, with Keri Russell as the mother whose daughter wanders into the woods, Alden Ehrenreich and O'Shea Jackson Jr. as low-level dealers, Ray Liotta in his final role as their kingpin boss, plus Brooklyn Prince, Isaiah Whitlock Jr., Margo Martindale, and Matthew Rhys. Sean and Amanda saw it together and never laughed once. They land on a convoluted, tonally lost kill comedy that's more gore than gag, with a bad CGI bear. Sean's most damning note: the movie is dedicated to Liotta, which he calls disrespectful.

segments.heading: Cocaine, the movie drug
segments.note: why coke plays like a period piece now

films.title: Easy Rider
films.note: Sean's entry point for the whole conversation: the New Hollywood landmark is, at bottom, a road trip to sell cocaine. He's never really loved it, but reads selling illicit product as a recurring American story engine, sometimes the drug itself, sometimes just the MacGuffin.

films.title: Goodfellas
films.note: The shared No. 1. Amanda knew she needed a Scorsese and picked the one whose helicopter-overhead paranoia and Henry Hill's cocaine spiral taught a generation both the glamour and the wreckage of the drug.

films.title: Basic Instinct
films.note: People doing cocaine and having sex around a lot of mirrors: the sleaze vibe Amanda imagined the '80s were, even though it's 1992. Sharon Stone, the ice pick, and a long tangent about the perfect untraceable murder.

films.title: American Psycho
films.note: Mary Harron's adaptation of Bret Easton Ellis. When Amanda hears 'cocaine in the '80s' she hears Ellis: the ultimate yuppie satire, with Christian Bale bringing full coke-eyed mania as Patrick Bateman.

films.title: Bachelorette
films.note: Leslie Headland's vicious comedy about three bitchy friends doing coke and being awful to the bride. Amanda's contemporaneous pick (she likes it more than 'Bridesmaids') and the movie she thinks of for blow at a special event.

films.title: Triple Frontier
films.note: Amanda's 'fun' curveball, chosen after a family poll. Nobody snorts anything, but the mule that falls off the mountain lands them on a cocaine farm, and the aggro, life-ruined energy of the four men reads as pure coke to her.

films.title: Goodfellas
films.note: The shared No. 1. For Sean the film's first hour is elegant gangster glamour, then cocaine becomes the tool that shows everything coming apart. Growing up near people tied to the Pileggi world, he says it was practically part of his youth.

films.title: Magnolia
films.note: Paul Thomas Anderson's third feature. Not on the list because Melora Walters's character snorts it, but because the whole movie feels like it was made by someone on cocaine, from the camera work down to the editing. Sean's 'least annoying friend on cocaine.'

films.title: Climax
films.note: Gaspar Noé's descent into hell: a '90s French dance troupe's after-party where the drugs, cocaine among them, curdle into purgatory. Sean nearly used it on the dance-movies episode. He calls it queasy and hypnotic, and says he didn't clock it as a period piece at first.

films.title: Scarface
films.note: Subbed in late because leaving it off would be stupid. Sean's granddaddy of the coke-opera gangster epic: De Palma at his height, an Oliver Stone script, and Pacino's most ludicrous performance in movie history.

films.title: Bug
films.note: Sean's curveball, not literally about cocaine but a one-to-one map of being consumed by a drug. William Friedkin directs Ashley Judd and Michael Shannon losing their minds together in a motel room. Genuinely upsetting, hard to rewatch.

films.title: Turning Red
films.note: Shi's Oscar-nominated Pixar feature about a Chinese Canadian girl who poofs into a red panda with her emotions. She pitched a universal puberty story through a culturally specific lens; it took eight screenings and rewrites with writer Julia Cho to land Ming's closing line, 'The further you go, the prouder I'll be.'

films.title: Bao
films.note: The Oscar-winning short that started as an indie side project, a dark, 'Chinese gingerbread man' riff on a mother wanting to consume her child. Shi pitched it to Pete Docter for feedback, and his enthusiasm pushed her into Pixar's open short-pitch process, where it won.

films.title: Inside Out
films.note: Shi's first full-time story-artist job out of her internship. She felt she'd lucked into a show where knowing the inside of a 13-year-old girl's head was currency. Docter's open story room, she says, built her early confidence and voice.

films.title: Incredibles 2
films.note: The feature 'Bao' premiered in front of. After it, Pixar invited Shi into development to pitch three feature ideas, the studio's home-grown path from short to director.

films.title: Aladdin
films.note: The first animated work Shi remembers falling for, her family's first VHS after immigrating to Canada. She credits it, half-joking, with an early awakening ('how is this possible, he's a drawing?') that fed into 'Turning Red.'

Ads: Netflix FIFA World Cup Launch Edition
